title: Pernicious anemia: parietal cells of the upper glandular layer
vignette: A 65-year-old woman comes to the emergency department with progressive fatigue and lower-extremity paresthesias. Medical history is otherwise insignificant and the patient takes no medications. Laboratory studies show a mean corpuscular volume of 112 fL. Upper gastrointestinal endoscopy shows atrophic mucosa in the gastric body and fundus and a normal-appearing antrum. A gastric body biopsy is labeled with four regions from superficial to deep: A (the superficial upper glandular layer), B (the deeper aspect of the glands), C (the muscularis mucosae), and D (the submucosa). Destruction of which of the following gastric layers is most likely responsible for this patient's symptoms?

clues.note: Anemia (fatigue) PLUS neurologic findings (paresthesias) is the hallmark of vitamin B12 deficiency. B12 deficiency causes subacute combined degeneration - demyelination of the dorsal columns and lateral corticospinal tracts - producing paresthesias and ataxia, which folate deficiency does NOT.
An elevated MCV (112 fL / normal 80-100 fL) is macrocytic anemia. Combined with neurologic signs it points to a megaloblastic, B12-deficiency anemia (macro-ovalocytes, hypersegmented neutrophils).

clues.text: atrophic mucosa in the gastric body and fundus and a normal-appearing antrum
clues.note: Atrophy limited to the BODY and FUNDUS with a spared antrum is the signature of autoimmune (type A) atrophic gastritis - pernicious anemia. Parietal cells live in the body/fundus, so this is where the autoimmune attack occurs (the antrum has none).

Parietal cells - the source of intrinsic factor (and HCl) - are concentrated in the superficial UPPER glandular layer of the oxyntic mucosa. Their autoimmune destruction eliminates intrinsic factor, causing B12 malabsorption and this patient's symptoms.

whatMatters: Macrocytic anemia + neurologic paresthesias + body/fundus atrophy (antral sparing) = pernicious anemia from autoimmune destruction of PARIETAL cells. Parietal cells (intrinsic factor + HCl) reside in the superficial UPPER glandular layer (region A). Losing intrinsic factor causes B12 malabsorption. Answer A.
strategy: Two steps. (1) Diagnose: macrocytosis + neuro signs + body/fundus atrophy = pernicious anemia (parietal cell loss -> no intrinsic factor -> B12 deficiency). (2) Localize parietal cells on the histology: they sit in the superficial/upper glandular layer (A). Chief cells (pepsinogen) are deeper (B); below are the muscularis mucosae (C) and submucosa (D) - structural, not secretory.

options.text: A - the superficial upper glandular layer
B - the deeper aspect of the glands

eliminations.label: B - deeper glands (chief cells), your pick
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: the deeper aspect of the oxyntic glands is rich in small, basophilic CHIEF cells that secrete pepsinogen. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: pepsinogen has nothing to do with B12 absorption; pernicious anemia results from destruction of PARIETAL cells (intrinsic factor) in the more superficial upper glandular layer. The trap is picking 'deeper glands' without recalling that parietal cells are more superficial. ✅ Correct if: a question about pepsinogen-secreting cells.

eliminations.label: C - muscularis mucosae (structural)
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: the thin smooth-muscle layer separating the lamina propria/mucosa from the submucosa. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: it has no secretory cells and no role in intrinsic factor or B12 absorption. ✅ Correct if: asked which layer demarcates mucosa from submucosa.

eliminations.label: D - submucosa (connective tissue)
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: well-vascularized connective tissue beneath the muscularis mucosae (containing the submucosal/Meissner plexus). ❌ WHY IT FAILS: it is structural/vascular, not the site of parietal cells - its loss does not cause B12 deficiency. ✅ Correct if: asked about the vascular connective-tissue layer or submucosal plexus.

reasoning: Macrocytic anemia (MCV 112) + neurologic paresthesias = B12 deficiency (subacute combined degeneration)
Body/fundus atrophy with antral sparing = autoimmune (pernicious) gastritis
Pernicious anemia destroys PARIETAL cells, which make intrinsic factor needed for B12 absorption
Parietal cells reside in the superficial upper glandular layer -> destruction of layer A; answer A
mechanism: Pernicious anemia is an autoimmune disorder in which cell-mediated (CD4-predominant) immunity destroys gastric PARIETAL cells, which are concentrated in the superficial upper glandular layer of the oxyntic (body/fundus) mucosa. Parietal cells secrete hydrochloric acid and intrinsic factor, a glycoprotein required for ileal absorption of vitamin B12 (cobalamin); their loss causes chronic atrophic gastritis (with oxyntic atrophy and intestinal metaplasia) and, once hepatic B12 stores are depleted, B12 deficiency. B12 deficiency produces a megaloblastic anemia (elevated MCV, macro-ovalocytes, hypersegmented neutrophils) and neurologic dysfunction from demyelination of the dorsal and lateral spinal tracts (paresthesias, ataxia). The deeper glands contain pepsinogen-secreting chief cells, and the muscularis mucosae and submucosa are structural layers, so none of those account for the B12-deficiency symptoms.

difficulty.label: 📚 Pure knowledge gap
difficulty.message: First Aid Heme/GI > pernicious anemia: autoimmune destruction of parietal cells (body/fundus); loss of intrinsic factor -> B12 deficiency -> megaloblastic anemia + subacute combined degeneration; chronic atrophic gastritis, intestinal metaplasia, gastric cancer risk; gastric gland histology (parietal = superficial/eosinophilic, chief = deep/basophilic).

difficulty.label: 🧭 Understanding the question
difficulty.message: The stem asks which LAYER's destruction causes the symptoms. Diagnose pernicious anemia, then localize parietal cells (intrinsic factor) to the superficial upper glandular layer, not the deeper chief-cell zone.

difficulty.label: 🔤 Vocabulary
difficulty.message: Unsure on parietal vs chief cells, intrinsic factor, oxyntic mucosa, muscularis mucosae vs submucosa, macrocytic/megaloblastic, or subacute combined degeneration? Tell me which.

difficulty.label: 🧲 Pulled by a distractor
difficulty.message: The deeper glands (B, your pick) hold chief cells (pepsinogen) - not the intrinsic-factor-making parietal cells. Parietal cells sit more superficially (A); that's the layer whose loss causes B12 deficiency.

difficulty.label: 🖼️ Imaging / diagram
difficulty.message: The histology labels A-D run superficial to deep. A = parietal-cell-rich upper glandular layer (large, pink/eosinophilic cells); B = deeper chief cells (small, basophilic); C = muscularis mucosae; D = submucosa. Identifying the superficial parietal-cell layer (A) is the whole question - anchor on 'parietal = superficial & eosinophilic.'

difficulty.label: ✍️ Something else
difficulty.message: Bank: pernicious anemia = autoimmune parietal cell loss (superficial upper glandular layer) -> no intrinsic factor -> B12 deficiency (macrocytic anemia + neuro); chief cells (deep) = pepsinogen.

keywords.front: Macrocytic anemia + paresthesias + body/fundus atrophy (antral sparing)
Where parietal cells reside in the gastric gland
keywords.back: Superficial upper glandular layer (oxyntic mucosa of body/fundus)
Hydrochloric acid and intrinsic factor (needed for B12 absorption)
Deeper aspect of glands; secrete pepsinogen
Subacute combined degeneration (dorsal columns + lateral corticospinal tracts): paresthesias, ataxia
Chronic atrophic gastritis -> intestinal metaplasia -> gastric adenocarcinoma

firstAid.detail: Heme/GI > autoimmune parietal cell loss (body/fundus, superficial glands); intrinsic factor loss -> B12 deficiency; anti-IF/anti-parietal antibodies.
firstAid.location: Heme/GI > autoimmune parietal cell loss (body/fundus, superficial glands); intrinsic factor loss -> B12 deficiency; anti-IF/anti-parietal antibodies.

firstAid.detail: Histo > parietal cells (superficial, eosinophilic), chief cells (deep, basophilic), muscularis mucosae, submucosa.
firstAid.location: Histo > parietal cells (superficial, eosinophilic), chief cells (deep, basophilic), muscularis mucosae, submucosa.

firstAid.detail: Heme > megaloblastic anemia (high MCV, hypersegmented PMNs); subacute combined degeneration; high methylmalonic acid + homocysteine.
firstAid.location: Heme > megaloblastic anemia (high MCV, hypersegmented PMNs); subacute combined degeneration; high methylmalonic acid + homocysteine.

memoryHook: Parietal cells = PARtial way down, up top (superficial) and Pink. They make intrinsic factor; autoimmune attack on that top layer (A) starves you of B12.
